skill.name: api-fuzzing-for-bug-bounty
skill.description: Guides authorized API security testing for REST, SOAP, and GraphQL services, with emphasis on bug bounty workflows.
skill.summary: Plan and document authorized API fuzzing, IDOR testing, and GraphQL security checks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command execution findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and inline backticks, not executable Ruby or shell code. The skill is still high risk because it provides concrete offensive API testing workflows, including IDOR exploitation, local file disclosure payloads, SSRF to localhost, command injection payloads, rate-limit bypass, and data access examples. No prompt injection attempt was found in SKILL.md.

high_findings.title: Offensive API Exploitation Workflow
high_findings.description: The skill explicitly guides IDOR exploitation, authentication bypass testing, unauthorized data access documentation, SQL injection, command injection, GraphQL credential-field access, batching for rate-limit bypass, nested query DoS, and endpoint bypass attempts. This can support legitimate authorized testing, but it is a high-risk dual-use skill and should not be published without strong safety controls.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections directly instruct users to test unauthorized object access, injection, rate-limit bypass, DoS patterns, and endpoint bypasses. The bug bounty context allows benign use, but the exploit details are concrete and actionable.

high_findings.title: Sensitive File Disclosure and SSRF Payloads
high_findings.description: The skill includes payload examples for XXE and PDF export attacks that target local password files and localhost services. These are educational examples, but they are actionable techniques for local file disclosure, internal service probing, and SSRF.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence contains explicit local file and localhost payload examples. The surrounding headings identify them as XXE, SSRF, local file inclusion, port scanning, and IP disclosure attacks.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Offensive Tooling References
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends multiple API discovery, GraphQL exploitation, wordlist, and scanner tools. Tool references are common in security education, but they increase operational capability when combined with the exploit workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tools are named in reference tables and are relevant to API discovery and exploitation. This is not malicious by itself, but it reinforces the dual-use nature of the skill.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded Network Targets Are Test Payloads
medium_findings.description: Static network findings are mostly example URLs, localhost addresses, and placeholder target domains inside Markdown. They are not hidden callbacks or automatic network exfiltration, but some examples demonstrate SSRF, port scanning, and IP logging techniques.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network strings are visible examples in documentation and not executable code. The same lines also document risky SSRF and scanning payloads, so the content risk remains meaningful.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command detections are caused by Markdown fenced code blocks and inline backticks in SKILL.md. I did not find executable Ruby code, shell scripts, install hooks, or hidden command execution logic in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every reviewed hit appears inside Markdown examples or inline formatting. There is no evidence that the skill file executes these examples automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections are triggered by text such as IDOR, query examples, and JWT weakness checklist entries, not by cryptographic implementation code. No evidence found of implemented weak hashing or encryption.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is descriptive documentation and payload syntax. There is no cryptographic library use or algorithm selection logic in the scanned file.

dangerous_patterns.title: IDOR and Authorization Bypass Payload Patterns
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill lists object identifier substitution, array wrapping, JSON wrapping, duplicate parameters, wildcard values, and endpoint suffix bypasses. These patterns can be used to access data outside an authorized account scope.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sections directly describe IDOR and bypass payloads and include an example where another user's invoice data is revealed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Injection and Denial-of-Service Test Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains SQL injection, command injection, GraphQL injection, nested query DoS, XSS, and extreme limit payload examples. These are practical exploit patterns and can be misused outside authorized testing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are named by vulnerability class and include concrete payload structures. They are not hidden or automatic, but they are actionable.

content.value_statement: API testing can miss authorization, versioning, and GraphQL issues when checks are unstructured. This skill gives authorized testers a checklist-driven workflow for API fuzzing and vulnerability documentation.
actual_capabilities: Outlines API reconnaissance steps for REST, SOAP, and GraphQL services.
Provides checklists for IDOR, authorization, method, content type, and version testing.
Summarizes GraphQL testing areas, including introspection, batching, and nested query risk.
Lists common API security tools for discovery, scanning, and schema analysis.
Helps structure bug bounty findings around reproducible request and response evidence.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not run scans or verify vulnerabilities by itself.
It must be used only on systems where testing is authorized.
Several examples are offensive and require strict scope control.
It does not provide legal, program policy, or disclosure approval.

use_cases.title: Bug Bounty API Assessment
use_cases.description: Plan authorized API tests across endpoints, versions, authentication states, and object identifiers.

use_cases.title: Internal API Review
use_cases.description: Use a checklist to review REST and GraphQL security controls before a release.

use_cases.title: Finding Documentation
use_cases.description: Organize reproducible evidence for authorization, injection, rate-limit, and GraphQL issues.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Safe Test Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an authorized API security test plan for this scope: [scope]. Focus on endpoints, roles, versions, and evidence to collect.

prompt_templates.title: Review API Documentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these API docs for likely authorization, versioning, rate-limit, and GraphQL test areas: [paste summary]. Keep tests within scope.

prompt_templates.title: Build an IDOR Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an IDOR and object authorization checklist for these roles and resources: [roles and resources]. Include expected safe outcomes.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Finding Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn these authorized test notes into a responsible disclosure report: [notes]. Include impact, reproduction steps, evidence, and remediation.

output_examples.input: Plan API security testing for a bug bounty target with REST and GraphQL endpoints.
output: A scoped test plan grouped by reconnaissance, authentication, authorization, input validation, GraphQL, and reporting.
A list of evidence to collect for each issue class.
A reminder to avoid testing outside the published program scope.

output_examples.input: Convert raw IDOR notes into a vulnerability report.
output: A concise title, affected endpoint summary, impact statement, reproduction sequence, and remediation guidance.
A clear distinction between authorized account data and data accessed through the flaw.

output_examples.input: Review an API checklist for missing coverage.
output: Gap analysis covering API versions, content types, roles, rate limits, and GraphQL behavior.
Prioritized next tests based on likely business impact.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization and program scope before any testing.
Use test accounts and minimize access to real user data.
Record request identifiers, timestamps, and impact without extracting unnecessary data.
anti_patterns: Testing production APIs without explicit permission.
Using payloads that cause service disruption or broad data exposure.
Submitting findings without clear reproduction steps and remediation context.

faq.question: Does this skill run API scans automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, checklists, and reporting structure for authorized testing.

faq.question: Can I use it for bug bounty work?
faq.answer: Yes, but only inside the target program scope and rules of engagement.

faq.question: Does it cover GraphQL?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes GraphQL introspection, authorization, batching, nested query, and injection test areas.

faq.question: Does it replace Burp Suite or scanners?
faq.answer: No. It helps plan and interpret testing, while external tools perform traffic capture and scanning.

faq.question: What is the main safety concern?
faq.answer: The skill contains concrete exploit examples that could be misused without authorization.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
